Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty inconsistent. I tried to set aside time on weekends mostly, maybe a few hours here and there during the week when I could manage. With limited resources at home, I often had to plan around when I could access reliable internet or find a quiet space. I'd say I aimed for about 15 hours a week, but it didn't always happen in a structured way. I'm more of a visual learner, so I spent a lot of time with diagrams and charts rather than just reading text.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot depending on what was due. I wish I could say I had a consistent schedule, but life kept getting in the way. When deadlines approached, I'd cram more hours in, but during quieter weeks, I might barely touch the course materials. My anxiety made it hard to stick to routines because I'd sometimes avoid studying when I felt overwhelmed.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline reminder or seeing a notification about something new posted. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there was anything I'd missed, especially since my attendance in actual classes was quite low. The gamified tasks they introduced actually got me curious a few times, which was surprising because I don't normally use educational technology much.
Q4: I can't say the LMS ever became a real habit for me. There were maybe a couple of weeks where I logged in more regularly when the gamified activities were happening because they made things feel less heavy. But mostly, I'd go through phases where I'd forget about it for days, then suddenly remember and try to catch up. It never felt natural or automatic.
Q5: The video content was probably the most useful for me because I learn best when I can see things explained visually. I spent quite a bit of time watching those videos, pausing and rewinding to understand concepts. The discussion posts were also helpful when other students explained things in different ways than the lecturer. Anything with diagrams or visual representations really stuck with me better than plain text readings.
Q6: I often ignored the lengthy reading materials and some of the optional supplementary resources. With my anxiety, looking at huge blocks of text would make me feel overwhelmed before I even started. I also didn't engage much with some of the peer interaction features because I felt shy about putting my thoughts out there. If something wasn't clearly connected to an assignment, I probably skipped it.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to my assignments or exams. The gamified tasks were surprisingly engaging because they broke things into smaller, manageable pieces and gave me that little boost of accomplishment. If something was too long or unclear about its purpose, my motivation would drop quickly. I needed to feel like I was making progress, not just going through motions.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't always afford. With my low motivation and the anxiety I deal with, I had to prioritize what was absolutely necessary. Required activities came with consequences, so they got done even when I didn't feel like it. Optional ones, even if interesting, often fell to the bottom of my list because the pressure wasn't there to push me through my avoidance.
Q9: Yes, definitely. Once I watched a video or downloaded a PDF, I'd often study from those offline because my internet isn't always reliable. I made handwritten notes with lots of diagrams and color-coding since that's how I process information best. I also borrowed textbooks from friends when I could and used those alongside the course materials. A lot of my actual learning happened away from any screen.
Q10: There were many times I'd be sitting with my handwritten notes, going over diagrams I'd drawn, or reviewing concepts in my head while doing other things. None of that shows up anywhere in the system. I'd sometimes discuss course content with classmates in person or over WhatsApp, working through problems together. The LMS only saw me when I was online, but my brain was working on the course material much more than that.
Q11: All the time, actually. I'd download videos when I had good internet access and watch them later when connectivity was poor. Same with PDFs and lecture slides – I'd save them and review them multiple times offline. It was more practical for me given my situation, and honestly, I could focus better without the distraction of being online. The LMS would show one download, but I might have studied that material five different times.
Q12: There were definitely harder periods, especially when my anxiety spiked. Sometimes I'd look at all the work piling up and just freeze, unable to start anything. When other responsibilities at home demanded attention or when I was worried about money and resources, the course felt like too much. Those weeks, I'd barely log in even though I knew I was falling behind, which only made the anxiety worse.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was exhausted or stressed about things outside school, the LMS was the last place I wanted to be. Confusion about what was expected in certain modules made me avoid them entirely sometimes. I'd tell myself I'd figure it out later, but later kept getting pushed back. My competing responsibilities often won out because they felt more immediate than coursework.
Q14: Yes, there were times I'd scroll through materials or have a video playing but my mind was completely elsewhere. I'd be too anxious to actually absorb anything, just going through the motions to feel like I was doing something. Sometimes I'd read the same paragraph multiple times without it registering. Those sessions probably showed up as engagement, but I wasn't really learning anything.
Q15: I postponed things constantly, especially when they felt overwhelming. Big assignments or complex topics would sit untouched because starting them triggered my anxiety. I knew they were important, but knowing and doing are different things when your mind is fighting you. Sometimes I'd wait until the pressure of the deadline was worse than the anxiety of starting, which wasn't a healthy pattern.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my main motivator, as embarrassing as that is to admit. I'd suddenly become very active on the LMS in the days before something was due. The gamified tasks helped a bit because they created smaller, more frequent deadlines that didn't feel as crushing. But mostly, I operated in crisis mode, which the LMS probably shows as spikes of activity followed by silence.
Q17: When I got good feedback, especially on assignments where I scored well, it gave me a temporary boost in confidence. I'd feel more willing to engage with the next task. But if feedback was critical or I didn't understand it, I'd sometimes avoid logging in for a while because I didn't want to face more disappointment. Feedback was powerful but could go either way for me emotionally.
Q18: The structure was a mixed bag for me. Having clear modules helped me know what to focus on, but the pacing sometimes felt too fast for my situation. The gamified elements they introduced actually helped break things into smaller chunks, which suited my anxiety better. When the structure was flexible, I appreciated it, but I also needed more guidance than I sometimes got.
Q19: Visual content and the gamified tasks made engagement easier because they matched how I learn and gave me achievable goals. What made it harder was when there were too many text-heavy resources or when expectations weren't clearly explained. My low engagement with educational technology in general meant the LMS itself felt clunky to navigate sometimes. Simpler, more visual design would have helped me a lot.
Q20: I think sometimes students might be searching for something specific or trying to understand what's expected without actually being ready to produce work. Maybe they're anxious like me and keep checking things without committing to action. Or they could be genuinely engaged but struggling with the actual execution of assignments. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity.
Q21: That actually sounds like me in some ways. My attendance and LMS activity were low, but I still passed with decent assignment scores. I did a lot of learning offline with downloaded materials and handwritten notes. The system couldn't see me reviewing my diagrams at home or discussing concepts with friends. Someone looking at my logs might think I wasn't trying, but I was working in ways that didn't register.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you're active, what you click on, and whether you submit things on time. But they miss the quality of that engagement and all the offline work students do. They can't measure understanding, anxiety, or genuine effort. For someone like me who learns visually and works offline a lot, the logs probably paint an incomplete picture of my actual involvement with the material.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just getting through it. It's when I'm curious about a topic and want to dig deeper, when I'm making connections between concepts. For me, engagement looks like drawing diagrams, asking questions, and thinking about the content even when I'm not actively studying. It's mental and emotional investment, not just time spent logged in.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I struggled with anxiety while trying to engage, or how the gamified tasks genuinely lifted my mood and made me feel capable. It wouldn't capture my offline study sessions, my handwritten notes full of diagrams, or the conversations with classmates that helped me understand difficult concepts. It also wouldn't show that despite my low visible engagement, I was surprised by how much I actually learned and how passing this course meant a lot to me given my circumstances.
